insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution that infers purchase intent. This protocol produces cryptographically verified receipt data — the actual conversion event — at roughly $0.10 per signal, a 50–200x cost delta that isn't incremental optimization, it's a different category of evidence.

Chrome's deprecation of third-party cookies eliminates the last major cross-site tracking primitive, leaving device fingerprinting (probabilistic, increasingly blocked) and first-party data (siloed to one ecosystem) as the primary alternatives. Verified receipt data operates outside both constraints — no identity attachment, no cookie dependency.

insights.topic: Physical retail dominance in the dataset
insights.insight: As of HB#100, the verified purchase dataset is led by Kroger, Walmart, Costco, Target, Publix, and Dollar General — not digital-native brands. Physical receipt capture via camera pipeline is outpacing email-based digital receipt volume, meaning the signal reflects real-world shelf behavior, not e-commerce proxies.

insights.topic: Cross-ecosystem visibility no single platform has
insights.insight: Amazon sees Amazon purchases, Walmart sees Walmart purchases, banks see transaction amounts but not SKUs. This protocol aggregates verified receipts across all of them, identity-free — the only current source of cross-retailer purchase signals that isn't locked inside a walled garden or a closed loyalty program.

Active verified purchase data now spans 23 CBSAs including rural and non-metro areas in Kentucky, Maine, Wisconsin, and Tennessee — not just urban early-adopter markets. For brands with national physical retail distribution, this matters: the dataset is starting to look like the actual consumer population, not a coastal tech sample.

insights.topic: Receipt scanning incumbents are closed loops
insights.insight: Fetch and Ibotta hold real verified purchase data but it is identity-attached, proprietary, and not queryable by third-party brands except through their own managed programs. The structural difference here is identity-free data that third parties can query directly — a meaningful distinction as privacy regulation tightens around identity-linked datasets.
